The lights flickered again. This time, they didn't come back on.

The clinic plunged into darkness.

Fang's phone screen was the only light. The camera feed showed the refrigerator door still open. The containers still trembling. And now, in the darkness of the basement, something was moving.

"The jars are breaking," the Taoist said.

"How do you know?"

"Because I can feel them." He pressed his palm against his chest. "Every one. Every spirit. I carried them with me for years before they died. I can feel them leaving."

The live feed flickered. One by one, the containers in the refrigerator stopped moving. The glass jars went still. The titanium cylinders stopped hissing. The vacuum-sealed pouches deflated.

Then the glass began to crack.

First, *WANG, LIHUA*. A hairline fracture ran down the side of the jar. Formaldehyde leaked out. The skin inside—a full face, preserved in amber fluid—twitched.

Then *YANG, MEILING*. The seal on her cylinder ruptured with a sound like a gunshot. A puff of vapor escaped. The skin inside contracted, then relaxed.

Then all of them at once.

Thirty-seven containers ruptured. Glass shattered. Titanium groaned. Pouches split open. The formaldehyde flooded the refrigerator, pooling on the floor, running in rivulets toward the drain.

And then the skins began to move.

They crawled. They slithered. They dragged themselves across the basement floor with no muscles, no bones, no bodies. Just faces. Empty faces. Smiling. Frowning. Weeping. Laughing.

They moved toward the stairs.

"They're coming up," Fang said.

"They can't climb stairs," the Taoist said. "They have no limbs. They can't grip."

But they could flow.

The formaldehyde pooled at the bottom of the stairs. The skins floated on top of it. And as the liquid rose, the faces rose with it. One by one, they reached the top step. One by one, they pressed themselves against the door at the top of the stairs.

The door to the operating room.

The door to where they stood.

"Seal the door," Officer Chen said.

"With what?" Fang's voice was shaking.

"Anything."

Fang grabbed a metal tray and jammed it under the handle. It wouldn't hold. They both knew it wouldn't hold.

Mrs. Chen's face stopped weeping. It looked at the door. The skin beneath her eyes was already beginning to loosen.

"I can feel her," she said. "The one who wears my face. She's two blocks away. She's walking toward the clinic. She's looking in her compact mirror, and the reflection is smiling at her."

She opened her mouth. A sound came out. It was not a scream. It was a song.

A lullaby.

The same lullaby the skins were singing from behind the door.

*"Sleep, my child, sleep. The river will carry you deep. The boat will come when the moon is round. And the ferryman will make no sound."*

The door shook.

The metal tray bent.

The lock snapped.

And the door swung open.

There was nothing there.

No skins. No formaldehyde. No faces crawling across the floor.

Just the dark hallway leading to the stairwell.

"They passed through," the Taoist said.

"Through where?"

"Through the veil." He pointed at the floor. "They're in the walls now. In the pipes. In the wiring. They're everywhere."

Fang's phone buzzed one last time.

*Hello, Doctor Fang. We're inside your building. We're inside your walls. We're inside your patients.*

*We're coming for you.*

*And we brought friends.*

The Taoist turned to him. His eyes glowed with the same green light as the cicada pendant.

"Your hour," he said, "is up."

Fang raised the scalpel.

"I'm not going down without a fight."

"That's not your choice," the Taoist said.

And then the lights came back on.

But the sockets were wrong.

Every bulb glowed green. Every shadow flickered. Every reflection showed something else looking back.

The mirrors in the clinic—the ones Fang had installed to help his patients see their new faces—were no longer showing the room.

They showed a river.

A wide, slow river under a moonless sky.

And on the other side, faces.

Hundreds of faces.

Waiting.

"Where are they?" Officer Chen's voice was steady, but his hand shook on his weapon.

"Everywhere," the Taoist said. "And nowhere."

He looked at Fang.

"Your clinic is a bridge now. Between the living and the dead. And they're crossing it."

"Can you stop them?"

"Can you give them back what you took?"

Fang looked at his hands. The hands that had performed hundreds of procedures. The hands that had peeled skin from dead faces and stitched it onto living ones.

"No," he said.

"Then no," the Taoist said. "I can't stop them. I can only guide them."

"Where?"

"To you."

The mirrors shattered.

Glass exploded inward. Shards flew across the room. Fang threw up his arms to protect his face. When he looked down, he saw his own reflection in a piece of mirror at his feet.

But it wasn't his face.

It was younger. Softer. A girl's face. The girl from the karaoke bar.

*Hello, Doctor Fang.*

Her lips moved.

*I'm going to wear your face now.*

The lights died again.

And in the darkness, something cold touched Fang's cheek.

It felt like skin.

His own skin.

Peeling away.

title: The Basement Screams
content:
The refrigerator hummed.

It was a perfectly ordinary sound—the same white noise that had filled Fang's basement for three years. The same vibration that had lulled him into believing he was safe, that his collection was secure, that the dead could be kept in jars like pickled vegetables.

But now the hum had changed.

It had developed a rhythm. A pulse. A heartbeat beneath the metal.

Fang stood at the top of the basement stairs, one hand on the door handle, the other still gripping the bloody scalpel. Behind him, the examination room had gone silent. The Taoist had not moved. Mrs. Chen's face had stopped laughing. Even the static had died.

The only sound was the refrigerator.

*Thump. Thump. Thump.*

Not mechanical. Organic.

The Taoist's beads clicked once. "They know you're here."

"Stay back." Fang's voice cracked. He didn't recognize it. "I'm warning you."

"You're warning me?" The Taoist almost laughed. "Doctor, there are thirty-seven angry spirits in your basement. They've been trapped in jars for months. Some of them for years. They've been waiting for this moment. And you're warning *me*?"

*Thump. THUMP. THUMP.*

The door vibrated.

Fang's phone buzzed.

He didn't want to look. But his hand moved on its own, raising the screen to his face. The message was from an unknown number. No contact name. No profile picture.

Just a single line of text:

*The last thing I saw was your face.*

Fang dropped the phone.

It clattered against the concrete floor. The screen cracked. But the message remained visible, glowing in the darkness like a wound.

"Who sent this?" he demanded.

The Taoist stepped closer. "All of them. They're sharing bandwidth now. They've learned to communicate through the grid. Through phones. Through cameras. Through anything with a signal."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" The Taoist pointed at Mrs. Chen. She had gone still. Too still. Her body was upright, her eyes closed, her mouth slack. But her face—the new face, the stolen face—was watching them from behind her eyelids. "You've been stealing their skins and grafting them onto living women. You've been creating hybrid creatures. Half-dead. Half-alive. You've been building bridges between worlds, Doctor. And now everything is crossing over."

The refrigerator door buckled.

Once.

A dent appeared in the center, pushing outward like a fist was trying to escape from inside.

Twice.

The lock snapped.

The door swung open.

Fang had designed the refrigerator himself. Custom-built. Ten feet tall. Eight feet wide. Titanium racks that could hold fifty jars at optimal temperature. Each jar was labeled with a patient's name, the date of harvest, and a number rating the quality of the skin.

He had been proud of that system.

Now the jars were moving.

They vibrated on their racks. The liquid inside—a saline solution mixed with preservatives—sloshed against the glass. Some of the jars had cracked. Some had already shattered. The skin inside them was no longer floating peacefully. It was reaching. Stretching. Pressing against the glass like hands trying to break through a window.

"Close the door," Fang whispered.

The Taoist didn't move.

"I said close the door!"

"You closed it three years ago, Doctor. When you took your first face. When you killed that girl in the karaoke bar and peeled her skin off in one perfect sheet. You closed the door then. And now it's open."

The first jar shattered.

It was number seven. A woman in her early thirties. Car accident victim. Fang had harvested her face before her family could claim the body. He had told himself she didn't need it anymore. She was dead. The skin was just meat.

But the skin that crawled out of the shattered jar was not meat.

It was a face.

Full. Complete. Alive.

It dragged itself across the floor like a jellyfish, leaving a trail of viscous fluid behind. It had no body. No bones. No muscle. Just the epidermis, the dermis, the subcutaneous layer—all intact. All moving. All *aware*.

It stopped at Fang's feet.

And it looked up at him.

The face had no eyes. No ears. No hair. But it had a mouth. And the mouth opened.

"Remember me?"

The voice was wet. Gurgling. Like someone speaking through a throat full of water.

Fang kicked the face away.

It slid across the floor, hit the wall, and kept moving. It climbed the wall. It crawled up to the ceiling. It hung there, upside down, staring down at him with its empty eye sockets.

Another jar shattered.

Then another.

Then three more.

The basement floor became a sea of crawling skin. Faces without bodies. Mouths without lungs. They slithered over each other, merging and separating, forming temporary shapes that collapsed and reformed.

The Taoist stood in the center of the room. He did not move. He did not flinch. His beads clicked in a steady rhythm, and his lips moved in a silent prayer.

"You can't stop them," Fang said.

"I'm not trying to." The Taoist opened his eyes. "I'm trying to guide them. To give them a path. They've been lost for so long."

"They're monsters."

"They were people. People you killed. People you butchered. People you turned into products."

"I gave them purpose!" Fang's voice broke. "They were dead! Their faces were rotting in the ground! I gave them new life! I gave them a second chance!"

"You gave them to rich women who wanted to look young."

"Is that so wrong? Is that worse than letting beauty rot?"

The Taoist looked at him. Really looked. And for a moment, Fang saw something in the old man's eyes. Not anger. Not judgment. Pity.

"Beauty doesn't rot, Doctor. It fades. There's a difference. But you never understood that. You thought if you could stop the fading, you could stop death itself. You can't."

The faces were climbing now.

They crawled up the walls. They covered the ceiling. They dripped from the pipes. They slid across the floor in waves.

And they were all moving toward Mrs. Chen.

The woman stood in the center of the room, still as a statue. Her eyes were open now. But they were not her eyes. They were the eyes of every face in the basement. Every soul she had been forced to carry.

"Help me," she whispered. But it wasn't her voice. It was a chorus. Thirty-seven voices speaking through one throat. "Help me. Help us. Help us all."

The Taoist reached into his robes.

He pulled out a small wooden box. Carved. Lacquered. Ancient.

Inside was a single piece of paper.

Yellowed. Frayed. Covered in calligraphy so dense it looked like a solid black rectangle.

"This is the contract," the Taoist said. "The original lease. The document that binds the dead to the earth. Every soul that passes through my hands signs this. It's their agreement. Their permission."

Fang stared at the paper. "What does that have to do with me?"

"You stole from me. You stole their skins. But you didn't steal their contracts. Which means you never owned them. You only borrowed them."

"Borrowed?"

The Taoist smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"Every face you harvested. Every jar in your refrigerator. They were never yours. They were held in trust. And now the trust is broken."

He held the paper up.

The faces stopped moving.

All of them. Every crawling piece of skin. Every slithering remnant. They froze in place, as if the entire room had been suspended in amber.

"The contract," the Taoist said, "has a clause. A final provision. If the borrower defaults—if the stolen property cannot be returned—then the borrower must forfeit an equivalent payment."

Fang's blood went cold.

"Equivalent payment?"

The Taoist looked at him.

"Your skin, Doctor. All of it. In exchange for theirs."

The faces began to move again.

But this time, they were not crawling toward Mrs. Chen.

They were crawling toward him.

Fang's phone buzzed one last time.

He didn't read the message.

But he saw the preview on the cracked screen:

*You wanted to be beautiful, Doctor. So did we.*

The first face touched his ankle.

Its mouth opened.

And it began to climb.

---

The lights went out.

The basement went dark.

And somewhere in the blackness, Fang heard the Taoist whisper:

"I'm sorry, Doctor. But this is the only way."

The door slammed shut.

The lock clicked.

And Fang was alone with thirty-seven pieces of skin that wanted to wear him.

The scalpel was still in his hand.

But he knew, with terrible certainty, that it would not be enough.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Basement Remembers

The vibration started as a low hum. Like a refrigerator cycling on. Like a phone set to silent, buzzing against a wooden surface. But it grew. It became a rattle. A shake. The sound of glass jars knocking against each other in the dark.

Fang felt it through the soles of his shoes.

The clinic's basement was not a basement. It was a converted storage room, temperature-controlled, humidity-monitored, with a lock that required both a key and a retinal scan. Fang had installed it himself, back when he believed that security meant keeping people out. He had never considered that the things he kept inside might want to leave.

The Taoist's beads clicked. Once. Twice. A rhythm that matched the vibration.

“How many?” he asked.

Fang did not answer.

“How many containers do you have down there?”

“I don't know.”

“Don't lie to me, Doctor.”

“I don't—” Fang stopped. His throat felt dry. The scalpel in his hand was suddenly very small. Very useless. “I stopped counting after thirty.”

The Taoist closed his eyes. His lips moved. A prayer. A curse. Something that made the air around him shimmer like heat rising off asphalt.

Mrs. Chen's new face watched them both. The tears had stopped. In their place was something worse: curiosity. The expression of a child watching ants drown in a puddle.

“Thirty-seven,” she said. Her voice was clearer now. Less static. More human. “There are thirty-seven of us in the cold. But there are more outside. The ones you didn't catch. The ones who saw what you did and ran.”

Fang's grip on the scalpel loosened. “I didn't—”

“You didn't what?” The face laughed. “You didn't mean to? You didn't know they were alive? You didn't realize that when you peel a spirit's skin, it feels everything? Every cut. Every tug. Every moment you stretch it over a needle and sew it onto a stranger's face.”

The lights flickered again. This time, they stayed dim.

“I know what you felt,” the face continued. “I was there. I was the first one. You were so careful. So precise. You made an incision along my hairline, just like you were trained. You used a scalpel with a number eleven blade. You told your assistant to hold my eyelids open so you could see the whites. You said—you said, ‘The eyes are the hardest part. They always look back.’”

Fang's hand trembled.

He remembered.

He remembered the karaoke bar. The girl. The way she had smiled at him from across the room, her voice raw and beautiful as she sang a song he didn't recognize. He remembered buying her a drink. Two drinks. Three. He remembered her laugh, loose and easy, and the way she had leaned into him when he offered to take her home.

He remembered the scalpel.

He remembered the way her skin slid off, like a silk dress falling from a shoulder.

“You were my first,” the face said. “But I was your favorite. You kept me separate from the others. A special jar. A special shelf. You came to visit me sometimes. You would open the lid and touch my face with your fingers. You would say, ‘You're perfect. You're the best one I've ever made.’”

The Taoist opened his eyes. They were wet.

“Doctor,” he said softly. “Tell me you didn't.”

Fang said nothing.

The Taoist's beads stopped clicking.

“I came here to help you,” he said. “I came here because I thought you were a fool who stumbled into something he didn't understand. I thought I could save you. I thought I could salvage this mess and guide those spirits across and let you go with a warning.”

He stepped closer. His face was inches from Fang's.

“But you knew. You knew from the start. You knew they were alive, and you did it anyway.”

“They were already dead,” Fang whispered.

“They were waiting!” The Taoist's voice cracked. It was not anger. It was grief. Pure, unfiltered grief. “They were waiting for me to guide them across. They were in the in-between. They could feel the wind. They could hear the music. They were one step away from peace, and you pulled them back. You pulled them into jars. You pulled them into needles. You pulled them onto the faces of women who paid you to make them young again.”

Mrs. Chen's face smiled.

“He's right,” she said. “We were almost there. I saw the light. I felt the warmth. And then I felt your scalpel.”

The vibration from the basement grew louder.

Fang's phone buzzed again. He didn't look at it. He didn't need to.

But the Taoist did.

He picked up the phone from the counter. His old fingers moved slowly across the screen. He read the message aloud.

“Doctor Fang, the face in my mirror just smiled. But I'm not smiling.”

He scrolled down.

“Doctor Fang, my skin is loose. I can feel it moving. It's like I'm wearing a coat that doesn't fit.”

Another.

“Doctor Fang, she's talking to me. The face. She's telling me things. Things about you. Things about the night you took her.”

The Taoist put the phone down.

“They're all connected now,” he said. “Every patient. Every face. They're all sharing the same nightmare. And it's spreading.”

Mrs. Chen's face nodded. “We're a family now. Doctor Fang made us a family. All thirty-seven of us, stitched onto living bodies, bound to beating hearts. We can't cross over. We can't leave. We're stuck inside women who wake up screaming every night.”

Fang's phone buzzed again.

Then again.

Then again.

It did not stop.

The Taoist looked at the screen. His face went pale.

“There are forty-three new messages,” he said. “From patients you haven't treated yet.”

“That's impossible,” Fang said.

“Is it?” The Taoist turned the phone toward him. “Look.”

Fang looked.

The messages were not from his patients. They were from strangers. Women he had never met. Women whose names he did not recognize.

But they all said the same thing.

*Doctor Fang, there's a face in my mirror. And it's wearing my smile.*

*Doctor Fang, I woke up this morning and I didn't recognize myself.*

*Doctor Fang, she's here. She's inside me. And she says she knows you.*

The Taoist put the phone down.

“They're spreading,” he said. “The spirits are reaching out. They're finding new hosts. Women who are afraid. Women who are lonely. Women who are desperate enough to listen to a voice in their head that promises to make them beautiful.”

Mrs. Chen's face laughed again.

“We're not just in your patients anymore, Doctor. We're everywhere. Every time a woman looks in a mirror and hates what she sees, we whisper to her. We tell her we can fix it. We tell her we can make her perfect.”

Fang's legs gave out.

He fell to his knees on the cold tile floor. The scalpel clattered beside him. His hands were shaking. His whole body was shaking.

“I didn't mean for this,” he said. “I just wanted—I just wanted to make them beautiful.”

“You wanted to make yourself rich,” the Taoist said. “Don't dress it up in poetry.”

The basement door rattled.

It was a heavy door. Steel-reinforced. Locked with both a key and a retinal scan. But it rattled like a screen door in a hurricane.

“They're coming,” Mrs. Chen's face said. “All of them. They want to meet you, Doctor. They want to thank you for keeping them so safe.”

The Taoist reached into his robes. He pulled out a string of wooden beads. Not the one on his wrist. A longer one. Older. The beads were black, polished smooth by centuries of fingers.

“I can hold them,” he said. “But not for long. Not with thirty-seven of them. And not with the ones that are already loose.”

He looked at Fang.

“You have two choices, Doctor. You can stay here and let them tear you apart. Or you can run.”

“Run where?”

“Anywhere. But you won't get far. They'll find you. They'll always find you. You're bound to them now. Blood to blood. Skin to skin.”

The basement door rattled harder.

A crack appeared in the steel.

Then another.

The Taoist began to chant. The beads clicked. The air grew thick. The temperature dropped so low that Fang's breath turned to fog.

Mrs. Chen's face screamed.

It was not a scream of pain. It was a scream of rage. Of hunger. Of a creature that had been waiting too long for its revenge.

“Run, Doctor,” the Taoist said. “Run while you still can.”

Fang scrambled to his feet.

He ran.

He ran through the clinic. Past the empty rooms. Past the mirrors that showed him a dozen different reflections of his own terrified face. Past the portraits on the wall, the before-and-after photos of women he had made young again.

Their eyes followed him.

Every single one.

He burst through the front door and into the Shanghai night. The streets were empty. The air was cold. His car was parked across the street, gleaming under a streetlight.

He ran toward it.

His phone buzzed again.

He ignored it.

He got into the car. Started the engine. Pulled away from the curb.

His phone buzzed again.

And again.

And again.

He looked at it.

The screen was black.

The phone was off.

But it was still buzzing.

He threw it out the window.

It landed on the pavement. The screen cracked. The buzzing stopped.

For one moment, there was silence.

Then his car's navigation system turned on.

A voice came through the speakers. Soft. Female. Familiar.

“Hello, Doctor Fang.”

His hands froze on the steering wheel.

“I've been looking for a ride.”

The face in the rearview mirror smiled at him.

It was the karaoke girl.

And she was not alone.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Collection Speaks

The vibration started as a low hum, barely perceptible beneath the buzz of the fluorescent lights. But Fang felt it through the soles of his shoes. A resonance. A rhythm. Like thirty-seven hearts beating in sync beneath his feet.

The Taoist's beads stopped clicking. His head tilted, listening. "They're awake now. You kept them in salt solution, didn't you? Preserved them like medical specimens."

Fang didn't answer. His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then again. Messages piling up like bodies.

*Doctor Fang, the skin on my arm is moving. Writing words. Chinese characters. I can't read them but my husband says they say "liar."*

*Why does my face smell like incense and rot? I washed it four times. The smell won't come off.*

*There's a woman in my reflection. She's not me. She's standing behind my reflection and she's pointing at me. Doctor Fang, please. Please tell me this is normal.*

The Taoist pulled out his own phone. A battered thing with a cracked screen. He held it up. "Look."

A live feed. Security camera footage from a hospital ward. A woman sat up in her bed. Her face was wrong. Not deformed—the surgery had been perfect—but the expression didn't match her eyes. She smiled, but her eyes wept. She laughed, but her throat produced a sob.

"She was your patient six months ago," the Taoist said. "Mrs. Zhao. Breast lift, facelift, eyelid surgery. You used three different spirits on her. Three different faces stitched together like a patchwork quilt." He zoomed in. "Watch."

Mrs. Zhao's smile froze. Her left eye twitched. Her right eye rolled back. Then her face split.

Not cracked. Not bled. Split. Cleanly, down the center, like a zipper opening. Two halves of her face peeled apart, revealing the muscle and bone beneath. But there was no blood. Because the face underneath—the real face, the one she was born with—was already there. It was like watching a snake shed its skin in reverse.

"Nurse ran out screaming," the Taoist said. "Security guard fainted. Mrs. Zhao is currently sitting in a puddle of her own detached face, asking for her husband. She doesn't seem to understand why he's crying."

Fang's hand shook. He pressed it flat against the table. "I didn't know this would happen."

"You didn't know? You've been stealing spirits for seven years, Doctor. You didn't think they'd eventually find a way to take back what you took?"

"They were dead. They didn't need their skin anymore."

"They were *waiting*. There's a difference." The Taoist pocketed his phone. "The spirits you harvested were supposed to cross over. Seventy-three years of accumulated karma, lifetimes of suffering, and they finally reached the threshold of release. But you cut them. You peeled them. You kept them in jars like pickled vegetables."

The face on Mrs. Chen had gone quiet. It watched the exchange with hungry eyes. "He doesn't understand," it whispered. "He thinks we're just meat. Just material. But we're *memory*. We're everything they lived. Their first kiss. Their children's births. The day they died. It's all in the skin."

Fang's phone buzzed again. He didn't look.

"You have to let them go," the Taoist said. "All of them. Every jar. Every container. You have to return what you took."

"And if I do?"

"Then maybe they'll stop hunting your patients. Maybe they'll move on. Maybe they'll find peace." He paused. "But probably not. You've bound them to living flesh. That's not easily undone."

The basement door behind Fang creaked open.

He turned. The staircase descended into darkness. But the darkness was moving. Shifting. Breathing.

"Your collection," the Taoist said. "They're coming up."

A sound from below. Wet. Slapping. Like meat being dropped on concrete.

Then a voice. Female. Multiple voices layered on top of each other, harmonizing and discordant at the same time. "Doctor Fang. We've been waiting so long."

The first hand appeared on the stair railing. Translucent. Blue-veined. The fingers wrapped around the wood and pulled.

A woman emerged from the darkness. She was naked. Her body was a patchwork of different skin tones. Pale. Olive. Dark brown. Sunburned pink. Stitched together with crude black thread. Her face was a mask of horror—seven different mouths sewn into the flesh of her cheeks, fifteen eyes scattered across her forehead and jaw, three noses stacked on top of each other like a totem pole.

She was beautiful in the way a car crash is beautiful. You couldn't look away.

"I'm the one you cut first," she said. Her mouths moved in sequence. One spoke. Then another. Then another. "I was seventeen. I died in a car accident. My parents donated my body to science. But you weren't science. You were a thief."

Fang's back hit the wall. "I didn't know. I didn't know the spirits stayed."

"You didn't want to know." She took another step. Her feet left wet prints on the tile. "You paid the coroner. You bribed the morgue director. You told yourself they were just bodies. Empty shells. But we were *in* there, Doctor. We were screaming. We were pounding on the inside of our own skin. And you didn't listen."

Behind her, more figures emerged. A dozen. Two dozen. Thirty-seven in total. Each one a puzzle of mismatched parts. Each one wearing the skin of multiple donors, stitched together into grotesque sculptures of human misery.

The Taoist's beads clicked faster. "They're manifestations. The spirits have gathered enough energy to form bodies from the scraps you left behind. The parts you didn't use."

"I used everything," Fang whispered.

"Exactly."

The patchwork woman reached the top of the stairs. She was close enough that Fang could smell her. Formaldehyde. Rot. The faint sweetness of decay.

"You kept me in a jar for three years," she said. "Three years in the dark. Floating in salt water. Listening to you walk past. Listening to you laugh. Listening to you take other women into your operating room and glue my sisters onto their faces."

"I'm sorry."

"Sorry doesn't give me back my skin." She raised her hand. The fingers were different lengths, different colors, different textures. "I had lovely skin. My mother said I had the complexion of a pearl. You cut it into seventeen pieces and sold it to seventeen women. They wear me on their faces while they shop and laugh and kiss their husbands."

The other spirits crowded behind her. Their eyes—hundreds of eyes, scattered across their bodies—fixed on Fang with an intensity that made his bones ache.

"We want our faces back," she said. "All of them. Every piece you stole."

"Some of them are gone," the Taoist interjected. "Absorbed into living tissue. You can't separate them without killing the hosts."

"Then the hosts die."

"No." Fang stepped forward. "No. The patients didn't know. They came to me because they were unhappy. Because they wanted to feel beautiful. They're victims too."

"Victims?" The patchwork woman laughed. Her mouths laughed in different keys. "They paid you to wear our corpses. They looked in their mirrors and smiled at our suffering. They are not victims. They are accomplices."

The lights flickered. The temperature dropped further. Frost formed on the windows.

"I can help you," Fang said. "I can find a way to separate them without killing anyone. I have resources. I have connections. I can—"

"You can give us back what you stole," the patchwork woman said. "Or we'll take it ourselves."

The Taoist stepped between them. "Enough. There's another way."

Everyone turned.

"The spirits are bound to the skin. But they're also bound to you, Doctor. You cut them. You processed them. You applied them. Your hands have touched every piece of them. Your blood is in their fibers." He held up his wooden beads. "I can transfer the binding. From the skin to you."

The patchwork woman's mouths went still. "What does that mean?"

"It means Doctor Fang becomes the vessel. Every spirit that's been wronged transfers their claim from the skin to his body. They live in him. They feel through him. They see through him." He looked at Fang. "You become the collection."

"And the patients?"

"They keep their faces. The spirits are extracted cleanly. No harm done."

The patchwork woman considered this. Her fifteen eyes blinked in sequence. "He would suffer."

"Immensely."

"Every day?"

"Every moment. The weight of thirty-seven lives. Their memories. Their pain. Their deaths. He would relive every one of them. Every day. For the rest of his life."

A long silence. The frost on the windows spread. The spirits murmured among themselves.

Then the patchwork woman smiled. It was a terrible sight—seven mouths stretching fifteen different ways. "I accept."

"No." Fang backed away. "No, I won't do this."

"You don't have a choice." The Taoist grabbed his wrist. The wooden beads touched his skin.

And the world went white.

---

*Pain.*

Not physical. Not emotional. Something else. Something deeper. The pain of being cut. The pain of being peeled. The pain of being separated from yourself and put in a jar and left in the dark.

*Seventeen years old. Car accident. The steering wheel went through her chest. She was alive for four minutes after the crash. Four minutes of watching her own blood pool around her. Four minutes of knowing she was dying. And then he came. The doctor. The one who took her skin while her body was still warm.*

Fang screamed. Or tried to. His throat wouldn't work.

*Twenty-three. Cardiac arrest during childbirth. The baby lived. She didn't. Her skin was perfect. Stretch marks on her belly but the face was flawless. He paid the coroner five thousand yuan for her face. He sold it to a real estate agent for fifty thousand.*

Another life flooded in. Then another. Then another. Thirty-seven lifetimes compressed into seconds. Every death. Every betrayal. Every moment of terror before the knife touched their flesh.

*He's in us now.*

The thought came from outside himself. From the spirits.

*We're in him.*

Fang opened his eyes. He was on the floor of his clinic. The Taoist stood over him, beads still glowing. The patchwork woman was gone. The other spirits were gone. Only Mrs. Chen remained, still unconscious on the operating table, her face—her real face—calm and intact.

"It's done," the Taoist said.

Fang looked down at his hands. They were shaking. But that wasn't what made him gasp.

His skin was moving.

Underneath his flesh, something writhed. Shapes pressed outward from inside. Faces. Hands. Eyes. They pushed against his skin from within, trying to get out.

"What have you done to me?"

"Given you a second chance." The Taoist knelt beside him. "You'll carry them. You'll feel them. You'll hear them every moment of every day. But no one else dies."

"How long?"

"Until you find a way to earn their forgiveness. If such a thing is possible."

Fang's phone buzzed. He didn't need to read it. He already knew.

*Doctor Fang, the face is gone. The face is gone and I don't know where it went

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Faces in the Walls

Fang's phone clattered to the floor. The screen cracked, but the message still glowed: *Doctor Fang, the face in my mirror just blinked. But I didn't blink.*

The Taoist didn't look at the phone. His eyes stayed locked on Mrs. Chen's twisting face—the one that wasn't hers anymore. The one that had started to speak in borrowed tongues.

"One hour," the Taoist said. "Tick-tock."

"You think I'm afraid of you?" Fang's voice came out steadier than he felt. "You're a fraud. A man in a costume, playing exorcist for rich women who don't know better."

The Taoist smiled. It was not a kind smile. It was the kind of smile that had watched empires crumble and monsters crawl out of their graves.

"Tell me something, Doctor Fang. When you cut that first spirit—the girl in the karaoke bar—did she scream? Did she beg? Did she offer you anything if you'd just let her go?"

Fang's jaw tightened. "She was already dead."

"Was she?" The Taoist stepped closer. "Or was she just stuck? Lost? Waiting for someone to show her the way home?"

Mrs. Chen's body went rigid on the table. Her arms snapped to her sides. Her back arched until only her heels and the crown of her head touched the surface. The face—the stolen face—opened its mouth wider than human anatomy should allow. The jaw unhinged. The lips peeled back. And from that impossible darkness, a sound emerged.

It was not a scream.

It was a song.

A Chinese pop song. Tinny. Distorted. As if played through a blown speaker in a cheap karaoke room.

"I can't find my way home," the face sang. "The lights are all wrong. The streets are all different. I've been walking for hours but the door won't open."

The Taoist's hand shot out. His palm pressed flat against Mrs. Chen's forehead. The singing stopped. The jaw snapped shut. But the eyes—still closed—began to move beneath the lids, tracking something only they could see.

"This is what happens when you steal a spirit's face," the Taoist said. "You don't just take their identity. You take their anchor. You take the thing that ties them to this world. And when you glue it onto someone else, you don't free yourself from the spirit. You weld yourself to its misery."

"I didn't know."

"You knew enough." The Taoist's voice went cold. "You knew they were spirits. You knew they weren't human. You just didn't care. Because they made you rich. They made you famous. They made you the man every woman in Shanghai wanted to see."

Fang's hand tightened on the scalpel. "I was helping people. Women who'd been told they were ugly their whole lives. Women whose husbands left them for younger models. Women who'd lost everything because of how they looked."

"You were helping them steal."

"Is that so different from what you do? You sell amulets. You perform rituals. You charge desperate people money to talk to their dead grandmothers."

The Taoist's beads clicked. "I don't charge."

"Then you're a fool."

"Maybe." The Taoist looked at Mrs. Chen. Her breathing had steadied. The stolen face had gone still. "But I've never had to sleep in a room full of jars."

The lights flickered again. This time, they didn't come back.

The room went dark. Not pitch black—there was still the glow from the hallway, the faint green light of the emergency exit sign—but dark enough that shapes lost their edges and faces became suggestions.

"I wouldn't move," the Taoist said.

"Why?"

Because something is standing behind you."

Fang didn't turn. He didn't breathe. He felt the cold—the sudden, unnatural cold that had nothing to do with the air conditioning and everything to do with the presence that had materialized at his back.

It spoke.

"You took my skin."

The voice was male. Deep. Rough, like gravel wrapped in wet leather.

"You took my skin and you gave it to a banker. A man who cheats on his wife. A man who laughs at beggars on the street. You gave him my face."

Fang's legs went weak. "I didn't know."

"You knew. You always know." The voice moved. It circled him. He could hear the footsteps, but they didn't sound like feet on tile. They sounded like wet meat slapping against concrete. "You knew when you cut me. You knew when you peeled me. You knew when you dropped my body in the incinerator and watched the flames turn my bones to ash."

"I had no choice. The spirit brokers—"

"—paid you well." The voice laughed. It was a horrible sound. Wet. Broken. Like a drowning man's last breath. "They paid you well, and you took their money, and you took my face, and now I walk the earth as a liar and a cheat."

The Taoist's voice cut through the dark. "Doctor Fang. Drop the scalpel."

"No."

"Drop it. Now."

"I said no."

The presence moved closer. Fang could smell it now—a mix of rot and smoke and something else. Something chemical. Something that reminded him of the formalin he used to preserve the skins in his collection.

"You want to know the worst part?" the presence whispered, its mouth inches from Fang's ear. "The worst part is that he knows. The banker. He knows the face isn't his. He sees me in the mirror every morning. He sees my eyes staring back at him. And he doesn't care. He likes the way I make him look. He likes the way women turn their heads when he walks by."

"He doesn't know you're real."

"Oh, he knows." The presence chuckled. "He knows because I've been talking to him. I've been whispering in his dreams. I've been showing him what I saw in my last moments. The knife. The table. Your face, hovering above me, calm and professional and utterly without mercy."

Fang's breath caught. "You can't—"

"I can. I have. And tomorrow, he's going to walk into your clinic and ask you why the face he's wearing belongs to a dead man."

The lights flickered back on.

The room was empty.

Mrs. Chen lay on the table, unconscious. Her face—the stolen face—was still. The Taoist stood by the door, his beads in his hand, his eyes fixed on a spot in the corner where the shadows seemed thicker than they should be.

"He's gone," the Taoist said. "For now."

"Who was that?"

"A construction worker. Fell off a scaffold three years ago. You took his face two weeks later. Sold it to a hedge fund manager named Liu Wei."

Fang's stomach turned. He remembered the case. He remembered the body—broken, bloody, the face still intact. He remembered the spirit broker's call. *Good specimen. Young. Healthy. Clean skin.* He remembered the money. Three hundred thousand yuan. The largest single payment he'd ever received.

"He's not the only one," the Taoist continued. "There are others. Thirty-seven, by my count. Thirty-seven spirits wandering the city, bound to faces that don't belong to them, trapped in bodies that can't see them, can't hear them, can't feel them."

"Thirty-six," Fang said.

The Taoist's eyes narrowed. "What?"

"I said thirty-six. There were thirty-seven containers. But one of them broke. Last night. I don't know how."

The Taoist's face went pale. "Which one?"

"I don't know. I didn't check."

"Show me."

Fang led him through the clinic. Down the hallway. Past the examination rooms. Past the storage closet where he kept the spare surgical supplies. To the door at the end of the corridor—the door that required a keycard and a code and a thumbprint to open.

The basement stairs creaked beneath their feet. The air grew colder. The smell of formalin grew stronger.

The refrigerator stood at the far end of the room. Stainless steel. Industrial grade. The kind used in morgues.

Fang punched in the code. The lock clicked. The door swung open.

The racks were empty.

Every single one.

The jars were gone. All twenty-seven of them. The containers that had held the skins—the faces, the hands, the patches of stolen flesh—were gone.

"No," Fang whispered. "I locked it. I checked it this morning."

"You checked it," the Taoist said slowly, "but did you check it?"

"What does that mean?"

"It means the spirits have been waiting. They've been patient. And now they've taken back what belongs to them."

Fang's phone buzzed again. He picked it up. Another message. Another patient.

*Doctor Fang, the face in my mirror just smiled. But I didn't smile.*

He scrolled down. There were more. Dozens of them. All from different patients. All saying the same thing.

*The face in my mirror just cried.*

*The face in my mirror just whispered my name.*

*The face in my mirror just asked me to help it find its body.*

*The face in my mirror just broke into a thousand pieces.*

*But I'm still looking at it.*

*And it's still looking back.*

Fang's hands shook. "What do I do?"

The Taoist looked at him. His eyes were old. Tired. Filled with a sadness that had seen too much death and too little justice.

"You find them," the Taoist said. "Every single one. You find the people who bought your stolen skins, and you take them back."

"And if they won't give them up?"

The Taoist's beads clicked. "Then you cut them off."

The basement lights went out.

The refrigerator door slammed shut.

And somewhere in the darkness, a voice—young, female, familiar—began to sing.

The same song. The same karaoke pop song. The same distorted, staticky recording.

But this time, it was closer.

This time, it was inside the refrigerator.

Fang pressed his ear to the cold metal. The singing was muffled. Distant. Like it was coming from very far away.

Or like it was coming from very deep inside.

"Let me out," the voice whispered, the song cut short. "Let me out, Doctor Fang. I want to see my face again."

The Taoist grabbed Fang's arm. "Don't."

"Don't what?"

"Don't open it."

"Why not?"

"Because she's not in there alone."

The refrigerator shuddered. The handle rattled. The lock clicked—once, twice, three times—and then the door swung open on its own.

The jars were back.

All twenty-seven of them.

But they weren't empty anymore.

They were full of faces.

And every single face was looking at him.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER SIXTEEN: The Refrigerator Opens

The vibration started as a low hum, barely perceptible beneath the fluorescence of the operating room lights. Then it grew. A rhythmic thumping, like a heartbeat amplified through concrete. The floor trembled. The instruments on the tray rattled against each other.

Fang knew that sound.

He'd heard it every night for the past six months, alone in his office, when the clinic went dark and the cleaners had gone home. The refrigerator in the basement had a pulse. He'd told himself it was the compressor. The coolant cycling through the pipes. The natural expansion and contraction of metal under temperature stress.

But he'd never believed it.

The Taoist's beads stopped clicking. His hand dropped from Mrs. Chen's face. He turned toward the floor, toward the source of the vibration, and his expression shifted. Not fear. Something older. Something that recognized the sound the way a soldier recognizes artillery.

"How many?" the Taoist asked.

Fang's throat had gone dry. "Thirty-seven."

"Thirty-seven containers. Thirty-seven spirits. And you kept them all in one place?"

"They were organized by date of harvest. Alphabetical by patient name. I have a cataloging system."

"A cataloging system." The Taoist laughed. It was not a happy laugh. "You built a library of stolen faces and you're proud of the Dewey decimal system." He grabbed Fang's wrist. "We have to move. Now."

"Move where? The refrigerator is in the basement. We're on the third floor."

"We need to get to the roof. High ground. Running water. Preferably a place with a clear exit route."

Mrs. Chen sat up on the operating table. Her new face—the face of the girl from the karaoke bar—had stopped weeping. It was listening. The eyes, still closed, tracked toward the door like a dog catching a scent.

"They're coming," she said. The voice was different now. Less human. More layered, like multiple people speaking through the same throat. "They're coming, and they're hungry."

"Shut up," the Taoist snapped. "You're not helping."

"I'm not trying to help. I'm trying to warn you. They don't just want their skins back. They want to wear yours."

The vibration intensified. Somewhere below, glass shattered. Then another. Then a cascade of breaking containers, one after another, like dominoes falling in a gallery of porcelain.

Fang's phone buzzed. Then again. Then a third time, the messages coming faster than he could read them. He glanced at the screen. Fifteen new notifications. All from patients. All with the same subject line: *It's moving.*

He opened one. A video. His patient—Mrs. Zhang, sixty-eight years old, post-procedure day four—staring into her bathroom mirror. Her new face was perfect. Smooth. Youthful. But her reflection was not.

The reflection was screaming.

Not her. The reflection. The skin on her mirror-double's face was peeling back in strips, revealing muscle and bone beneath. And beneath that, something else. Something that did not belong in a human body.

"Doctor Fang, please." Mrs. Zhang's voice was barely a whisper. "Please tell me what's happening. Please tell me this is normal."

The video ended.

Fang's hand trembled. He dropped the phone.

"Those women," he said. "My patients. The spirits are reaching them through the skin grafts. They're—"

"Possessing them," the Taoist finished. "Yes. That's what happens when you glue a ghost to a living person's face. The ghost learns to drive."

Mrs. Chen stood up. Her movements were wrong. Too fluid. Like a puppet whose strings had been oiled. She walked to the door, her bare feet leaving wet prints on the tile.

"I'm going downstairs," she said. "I want to see my sisters."

"Sisters?" Fang's voice cracked.

"The other faces. The ones you took. We were together in the dark for so long. We shared stories. We shared memories. We shared the pain of being cut away from our bodies." She paused at the threshold. "We're going to be together again. And then we're going to find our bodies. And then we're going to make you watch as we put ourselves back together."

She left.

The vibration from below stopped.

Silence. Thick and heavy and wrong.

"What happens now?" Fang asked.

The Taoist was already moving, grabbing Fang's arm, pulling him toward the stairwell. "Now we run. We get to the roof. We call for backup."

"Backup? You have backup?"

"I have colleagues. Other Taoists. Spirit hunters. People who deal with this kind of thing." He pushed open the stairwell door. "But they're an hour away. And we don't have an hour."

They descended. Two floors. The stairwell was dim, the emergency lights casting long shadows. Fang's footsteps echoed. The Taoist's beads clicked. The air grew colder with each step.

On the landing between the second and first floors, they stopped.

The door to the basement was open.

Light spilled out. Pale. Blue. The color of a dead television screen.

And from within, a sound. Wet. Slopping. Like someone walking through a puddle of mud.

"No," Fang whispered.

"Yes," the Taoist said. "They're out. All of them."

They moved past the basement door. Carefully. Quietly. Fang caught a glimpse of the interior. The refrigerator door was open. The titanium racks were empty. The containers—glass jars, plastic tubs, vacuum-sealed bags—lay scattered across the floor, their contents spilled.

The skins were gone.

But the skins had never been just skins. They'd been anchors. Vessels. The physical remnants of spirits that had been trapped between worlds, waiting for someone to guide them across.

Fang hadn't guided them. He'd harvested them. He'd bottled them. He'd sold them to wealthy women who wanted to look young again.

And now they were free.

They reached the first floor. The reception area was dark. The front doors were locked. Through the glass, Fang could see the street outside. Empty. Silent. The streetlights flickered.

"The power grid," the Taoist said. "They're affecting the electrical systems. They're drawing energy from the city."

"Can they do that?"

"They can do whatever they want. They're spirits who've been trapped in cold storage for months. They're angry. They're hungry. And they've had nothing to do but think about revenge."

The front doors rattled.

Not from outside. From inside.

Something was pushing against them from the other side. Something that wanted to get out.

"We can't go that way," the Taoist said. "Roof. Now."

They turned. The stairwell door was still open. But the light from the basement had grown. It was spreading up the stairs, crawling across the walls like a slow tide.

And from within the light, shapes began to form.

Faces.

Dozens of faces, pressed against an invisible membrane, pushing outward, desperate to break through.

"You can see them?" Fang asked.

"I can see them," the Taoist said. "You should be able to see them too. They're connected to you. You handled their skins. You cut them. You preserved them. You're part of them now."

Fang looked at his hands. The hands that had performed hundreds of procedures. The hands that had sliced through spirit flesh and sewn it onto living bone.

For the first time, he noticed the marks.

Thin lines. Pale. Raised. They ran across his palms, his fingers, his wrists. Like scars. But he'd never been cut there.

"They're inside you," the Taoist said. "Every time you touched a skin, a piece of the spirit entered your bloodstream. You've been carrying them with you for months."

"That's impossible."

"Is it? Look at your reflection."

Fang turned. The window beside the reception desk reflected the room. It reflected him.

But the reflection was wrong.

His face was moving. Not his real face—the reflection. It was smiling. But he was not.

"I'm going to ask you a question," the Taoist said. "And I need you to answer honestly. Did you ever wear one of the skins? Did you ever put one on?"

Fang's memory flickered. A night. Six months ago. Alone in his office. A jar on his desk. The skin of a young woman who'd died in a car accident. Perfect. Unblemished. He'd held it up to the light. He'd wondered what it would feel like to wear it. To be someone else for just a moment.

He'd pressed it against his face.

Just for a second.

Just to see.

"That's a yes," the Taoist said. "That's a definite yes."

The reflection in the window stopped smiling. It leaned forward. Its face pressed against the glass from the inside, distorting, stretching.

"You wore me," it said. "You wore me, and you liked it."

The lights went out.

Complete darkness. The kind that had weight. The kind that pressed against your eyes and filled your ears with the sound of your own heartbeat.

"Don't move," the Taoist whispered. "Don't speak. Don't breathe if you can help it."

Something touched Fang's shoulder.

Cold. Fingertips. Five of them.

He felt them press into his skin. He felt them grip his collarbone. He felt them pull.

The lights flickered back on.

The Taoist was gone.

The stairwell door was closed.

And standing in front of Fang, wearing a face he recognized, was a woman.

She was young. Early twenties. Long black hair. A mole above her left eyebrow.

She was the first one. The very first one. The girl from the karaoke bar. The one whose skin had started this whole nightmare.

"Hello, Doctor Fang," she said. Her mouth moved. But the voice came from everywhere. From the walls. From the ceiling. From inside his own skull. "I told you I'd come back."

She reached up and touched her face. Her fingers found the edge of her jaw. She began to peel.

The skin came away in one piece. Clean. Perfect. She held it in her hands like a mask she'd just removed.

Underneath, there was nothing.

No muscle. No bone. No blood.

Just a void. A darkness so complete it seemed to swallow the light around it.

"This is what you left me," she said. "This is what I've been wearing in the dark. Nothing."

She dropped the skin. It landed on the floor with a wet slap.

"Now I want yours."

The lights went out again.

And from somewhere in the darkness, Fang heard the Taoist's voice. Distant. Muffled. Like he was speaking through water.

"Dr. Fang. Don't let her touch your face. Whatever you do—"

The voice cut off.

And the darkness began to move.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Collection Speaks

The vibration started as a low hum, barely perceptible beneath the fluorescent hum of the clinic's lights. But within seconds, it grew into something else. Something rhythmic. Like thirty-seven hearts beating in unison.

Fang felt it in his bones first. The floor beneath his feet began to tremble. The surgical instruments on the tray clattered against each other. The glass cabinet behind him rattled, and he watched as a small vial of anesthetic walked itself to the edge of the shelf and fell, shattering on the linoleum.

The Taoist's beads clicked faster. His eyes never left the floor. "They know we're here."

"Impossible." Fang's voice cracked. "They're just skins. Preserved tissue. Nothing living."

"Nothing living?" The Taoist laughed without humor. "You've been harvesting the dead, Doctor. But you don't understand what you've been harvesting from. A spirit doesn't stop existing just because you peel away its vessel. You've been keeping their faces in jars like trophies. And trophies remember."

Mrs. Chen's face—the borrowed face—began to sing. It was a lullaby. High and thin and ancient. The melody crawled up the walls, seeped into the corners, found every crack in the room's silence.

"Stop that," Fang said.

"I can't." The face's lips moved independently of Mrs. Chen's jaw. "She's calling them. The one you killed first. The girl from the karaoke bar. She was young. She was strong. She's been waiting the longest."

The basement door was at the end of the hallway. Fang had reinforced it with a steel plate, installed a biometric lock, lined the interior with lead sheeting to block any kind of signal. It was his vault. His sanctuary. His grave.

Now it sounded like something was trying to claw its way out.

The Taoist grabbed Fang's arm. His grip was iron. "How many containers?"

"Thirty-seven."

"Total?"

"Thirty-seven patients. But some of them had multiple procedures. Touch-ups. Corrections. I kept extras."

"Extras." The Taoist's voice dropped to a whisper. "You kept extra skins. Like spares. Like—" He stopped. His eyes widened. "Oh, you fool. You absolute fool."

"What?"

"The binding doesn't just work one way. When a patient wears a spirit's skin, the spirit can feel through it. See through it. And if you kept extra pieces—" He turned toward the hallway. The banging had started now. Steady. Methodical. A fist against steel. "They've been building a map. Every time you cut a piece off and stored it separately, you gave them another anchor. Another point of connection. They don't just know where your basement is, Doctor. They know *everything*."

The banging stopped.

Then a voice came through the door. Clear. Calm. Familiar.

"Doctor Fang? It's Mrs. Patterson. From the Claymore procedure. Remember? You said I'd be beautiful again."

Fang remembered. Mrs. Patterson was sixty-three. She'd wanted to look forty. He'd given her the face of a twenty-eight-year-old accountant who'd died in a car accident. The skin had taken perfectly. She'd cried with joy.

"The Claymore skin was a gift," the voice continued. "But I think I'd like it back now."

The Taoist moved. Fast. Faster than a man his age should be able to move. He crossed the room, pulled a length of yellow silk from his sleeve, and pressed it against the doorframe. The silk glowed. The banging resumed, but it was muffled now. Distant.

"That won't hold them long," he said. "They're too close to the physical world. Too many anchors. You've been feeding them for three years, Doctor. Three years of stolen faces. They're not just spirits anymore. They're something else. Something hungry."

Fang's phone buzzed. Then again. Then again. Messages piled up. The screen filled with notifications.

He looked down.

*Doctor Fang, I think my face is trying to leave.*

*Doctor Fang, my reflection just waved at me. I didn't wave back.*

*Doctor Fang, she's talking to me. The girl in the skin. She says she knows you.*

*She says you told her it wouldn't hurt.*

The last message was a video. Fang opened it.

The video showed a woman—Mrs. Lin, he recognized her from the Tuesday procedure—sitting in her bathroom. Her face was calm. Her hands were in her lap. But her skin was moving. Rippling. Like something was trying to get out from underneath.

Then the skin on her cheek split open.

A mouth formed in the gap. A second mouth, smaller, younger, with teeth that didn't match Mrs. Lin's dental records. The mouth opened and spoke.

"Doctor Fang. I can see you through her eyes. She's looking at her phone. And you're looking back."

The video ended.

Fang dropped the phone. It clattered to the floor. The screen cracked, but the display still glowed, still showed the last frame of Mrs. Lin's face splitting open like a piece of rotten fruit.

"You see?" The Taoist's voice was quiet now. Almost gentle. "You've been wearing the dead. And the dead have been watching you back. Every time you looked at your work in the mirror, every time you admired a perfect graft, every time you touched their skin—they felt it. They've been patient. But patience ends."

"We can fix this." Fang's voice was desperate. "We can remove the skins. Reverse the procedures. I have—there are techniques—"

"There are no techniques." The Taoist shook his head. "You didn't graft skin, Doctor. You performed a possession. Surgical possession. The cleanest I've ever seen. The spirits didn't just attach to the flesh. They attached to the blood. The nerves. The soul. Your patients aren't wearing masks. They're sharing bodies with the dead."

"Then what do we do?"

The Taoist was silent for a long moment. The banging on the basement door had stopped. But the silence was worse. It meant they were planning.

"I can sever the bonds," he said finally. "But it will kill the spirits. Completely. Permanently. They won't cross over. They won't reincarnate. They'll just stop existing."

"And the patients?"

"They'll live. But they'll know. They'll remember everything. The moments they shared a body. The thoughts that weren't theirs. The memories of people they never met." He looked at Fang. "Some of them won't survive the knowledge."

Fang thought of Mrs. Chen, still unconscious on the table. He thought of Mrs. Lin, sitting in her bathroom with a second mouth in her cheek. He thought of the other thirty-four. The ones who hadn't called yet. The ones who were still unaware that they carried the dead inside them.

"Do it," he said.

The Taoist studied him. "You understand what you're asking? You're asking me to destroy thirty-seven souls."

"I'm asking you to save thirty-seven living people."

"Semantics." But the Taoist nodded. He reached into his robe and pulled out a small brass bell. It was tarnished, old, covered in symbols Fang didn't recognize. "I'll need access to your collection. Every piece. Every jar. Every scrap of skin you've hoarded."

"The basement."

"I know." The Taoist walked to the door. He paused with his hand on the frame. "When this is over, Doctor Fang, you will answer for what you've done. Not to me. Not to the law. To them. The ones you killed. The ones whose faces you stole. They will have their say."

Fang nodded. He didn't have words.

The Taoist opened the door.

The hallway was empty. But the air was thick. Heavy. Like walking through water. The lights flickered. The floor was wet, but Fang couldn't tell with what. Water? Blood? Something else?

The basement door was at the end of the hall. It looked normal. Steel. Biometric lock. Lead-lined. But the symbols on the Taoist's silk had burned themselves into the frame, glowing faintly, like embers.

"They've been trying to get through," the Taoist said. "But they're disorganized. Too many voices. Too many agendas. The girl from the karaoke bar—she's the strongest. She's been leading them. But the others are fighting for control."

"How do you know?"

"Because I can hear them." The Taoist tapped his ear. "Can't you?"

Fang listened. At first, nothing. Then, beneath the hum of the lights, beneath the drip of whatever was wetting the floor, he heard it. Whispers. Dozens of them. Overlapping. Arguing. Pleading. Demanding.

*He took my eyes.*

*He took my smile.*

*He took my youth.*

*He took my death.*

The voices grew louder as they approached the basement door. The silk on the frame was smoking now, curling at the edges. The symbols flickered.

"Hurry," Fang said.

The Taoist placed his palm against the door. The biometric lock beeped twice, then clicked open. The door swung inward.

The basement was cold. Colder than it should have been. The refrigerator hummed in the corner, its glass door fogged with ice. Inside, the jars sat in neat rows. Thirty-seven of them. Each one labeled. Each one containing a face.

But the faces were not still.

They pressed against the glass. They moved. They twisted. They opened their eyes.

One jar—the one marked "Karaoke Bar, 2019"—vibrated so hard that the glass cracked. A thin line spiderwebbed across the surface, and the face inside smiled.

"Hello, Doctor."

Fang took a step back.

"You've been busy," the face said. Its voice came from everywhere. From the jar. From the walls. From the space behind Fang's eyes. "But we've been busy too. Did you know that your patients can hear us now? All of them. We've been talking. We've been telling them what you did. How you found us. How you cut us. How you wore us."

"I didn't wear you." Fang's voice was barely a whisper. "I gave you to others."

"Same thing. We were still being worn. Still being used. Still being seen in mirrors that didn't belong to us." The crack in the jar widened. A thin trickle of liquid escaped, ran down the shelf, dripped onto the floor. "But you know what the worst part is, Doctor? The worst part is that we liked it. At first. Being seen again. Being beautiful again. Being alive again."

"Then why—"

"Because we remembered. Eventually. We remembered that we were dead. And being dead isn't so bad when you're at peace. But you took our peace. You took our faces. And now we want them back."

The jar shattered.

Liquid exploded across the basement, and the face—the face of a young woman, no older than twenty-two, with dark hair and dark eyes and a smile that was all wrong—flew through the air. It landed on the floor, wet and glistening, and began to crawl.

The Taoist rang the bell.

The sound was sharp. Clean. It cut through the whispers like a blade. The face stopped moving. The other jars went still.

"Enough," the Taoist said. "You will be silent."

The face on the floor opened its mouth. "You can't silence us, old man

title: The Basement Screams
content:
The first container shattered at 11:47 PM.

Fang heard it from three floors up, through concrete and insulation and the hum of the building's HVAC system. A sound like a wine glass hitting tile. Then another. Then a third, faster, the cracks coming in rapid succession like gunfire.

The Taoist's beads stopped clicking.

"How many did you say?" the old man asked.

"Thirty-seven," Fang whispered.

"Thirty-seven containers. Thirty-seven spirits. And you kept them in the basement."

"They were in a medical refrigerator. Temperature controlled. Sterile."

"You kept dead people's skin in a refrigerator." The Taoist's voice carried no judgment. Just exhaustion. The exhaustion of a man who had spent sixty years cleaning up messes made by people who should have known better. "Like leftovers."

Mrs. Chen lay on the operating table, her body rigid, her borrowed face frozen mid-scream. The skin had stopped moving after the Taoist touched her cheek. But her eyes—her real eyes, the ones that belonged to the sixty-three-year-old grandmother who had come in for a routine procedure—those eyes were wide open. And they were watching.

"What's happening to her?" Fang asked.

"She's trapped," the Taoist said. "Her consciousness is still in there. But the spirit's skin is bonded to her nervous system. Every time the spirit moves, she feels it. Every time the spirit speaks, she hears it in her own voice." He pressed two fingers to Mrs. Chen's temple. "She can see us. She can hear us. But she can't move. She can't blink. She can't even close her eyes."

The basement door slammed open.

The sound echoed up the stairwell, bounced off the walls, reached the third floor like a runner passing a baton. Fang's phone buzzed. Then his watch. Then the desktop computer in the corner of the room, the one he used for patient records, the one that had never been connected to anything except the clinic's internal network.

The screen flickered to life.

A video file opened automatically.

Fang recognized the footage. It was from the security camera in the basement hallway. He watched himself walk past the refrigerator at 2:14 AM, six months ago, carrying a stainless steel container labeled "Patient 14." He watched himself enter the storage room. He watched himself place the container on the rack.

The timestamp changed.

3:47 AM. The same night. The camera angle shifted to the inside of the storage room. The refrigerator door opened from the inside. A hand emerged. Pale. Thin. The fingernails were painted red.

Fang stepped back.

"What is that?"

"That's one of your patients," the Taoist said. "Patient 14. The one you killed in the karaoke bar. She was a singer. Twenty-three years old. She had a mother in Nanjing and a younger brother who still thinks she's studying abroad."

"I didn't kill her."

"You took her skin while she was still alive."

"I took her skin after she was dead."

The Taoist turned to face him fully. For the first time, Fang saw something other than calm in the old man's eyes. He saw anger. Old anger. The kind that had been simmering for decades, waiting for a moment like this.

"Who told you they were dead?"

Fang opened his mouth. Closed it. Opened it again.

"The spirits," he said slowly. "When I cut them, they don't scream. They don't bleed. Their hearts stop beating."

"Because you're cutting their skin off. You're separating them from their bodies. Of course their hearts stop—you're removing the thing that keeps them connected to this world." The Taoist stepped closer. "But they're not dead, Doctor Fang. They're trapped. Conscious. Aware. Floating in a void of their own making, watching you walk away with their faces."

The computer screen changed.

Another video. Another container.

Patient 9. A woman who had come to Fang for a facelift. He had harvested her skin in the operating room, under bright lights, with sterile instruments. He had sewed her face back on—a cheap synthetic substitute, the kind he used for all his patients—and sent her home with a smile and a bill for fifty thousand yuan.

She had called him three days later.

Screaming.

Saying her face was melting.

Saying she could feel something moving under her skin.

Fang had told her it was normal. Told her the swelling would go down. Told her to ice it. To rest. To take the painkillers he had prescribed.

She had called again the next day.

This time, she wasn't screaming.

She was whispering.

"Doctor Fang, there's a woman inside my face. She's trying to get out. She's telling me things. She's telling me what you did to her."

Fang had hung up.

He had blocked her number.

He had deleted her file from the system.

The refrigerator door in the video opened wider. The hand retreated. And then something emerged. Something that made Fang's stomach turn inside out.

It was a face.

Just a face.

No body. No skull. No support structure of any kind. A face, floating in the air, held together by nothing but memory and rage. The eyes were open. The mouth was moving. The skin was the color of old paper, stretched thin, translucent in places.

It was the face of the girl from the karaoke bar.

"I told you," the face said. Its voice came through the computer speakers, tinny and distant. "I told you I would find my way back."

The video ended.

The screen went black.

And from the basement, a sound rose up. Not breaking glass this time. Something worse.

Footsteps.

Many of them.

Walking in unison.

The Taoist grabbed Fang's arm. "How many containers were intact when you last checked?"

"All of them. I checked this morning."

"This morning. Before the first spirit woke up. Before Mrs. Chen's face started moving." The old man's grip tightened. "They've had twelve hours to figure out how to get out of those containers. Twelve hours to remember how to walk."

The footsteps grew louder.

Closer.

The stairwell door was at the end of the hallway. Fang could see it from where he stood. The metal handle. The push bar. The sign that said "EXIT" in red letters.

The handle began to turn.

Slowly.

Deliberately.

"There's a back stairwell," Fang said. "Behind the supply closet. Leads to the parking garage."

"You think that will stop them?"

"I think it will buy us time."

The door swung open.

The hallway lights flickered.

And the first figure stepped through.

It was Patient 9. The woman who had called him. The woman whose face he had stolen and replaced with synthetic skin. She looked exactly as she had the day she walked out of his clinic. Same clothes. Same hair. Same posture.

But her face was wrong.

The skin was too loose. It sagged at the edges, pooled around her jaw, folded over her eyes. She looked like she was wearing a mask made of her own face. A mask that was three sizes too big.

"Doctor Fang," she said. Her voice was wet. Gurgling. Like she was speaking through a mouth full of water. "I found her."

"Found who?"

"The woman inside my face. She's out now." She reached up and grabbed her cheek. The skin stretched. Pulled. Came away from the bone with a sound like Velcro. "She's walking around. She's wearing my body. And she's very, very angry."

Behind her, more figures emerged.

Patient 14. The singer. Her face was intact—Fang had harvested her skin cleanly, with precision, with care. But she wore it like a trophy. Like she knew it didn't belong to her.

Patient 22. A businessman who had wanted to look younger for his wedding. His skin was peeling at the edges, curling like old wallpaper.

Patient 31. A housewife whose face was split down the middle—half the synthetic substitute Fang had given her, half the original skin, the two halves fighting for control.

They filled the hallway.

Thirty-seven of them.

Thirty-seven bodies that had been hollowed out and filled with borrowed skin.

And at the front of the crowd, leading them, was Patient 9.

No.

Not Patient 9.

The spirit inside Patient 9.

The woman whose face had been stolen, whose consciousness had been trapped in a jar in a refrigerator, who had spent six months floating in darkness, waiting, watching, planning.

"Hello, Doctor," she said. Her voice was clear now. Strong. The voice of someone who had found what she was looking for.

"Hello."

The Taoist stepped forward. His beads clicked. Once. Twice. Three times.

"You're bound," he said. "All of you. You're bound to these bodies by blood and skin. If you try to leave, you'll tear them apart."

"We know," the spirit said. "We don't want to leave."

"Then what do you want?"

She smiled.

It was not a human smile.

The lips stretched too wide. The skin around the mouth pulled taut. The same smile Fang had seen on Mrs. Chen's face, an hour ago, in this same room.

"We want to show him what it feels like," she said. "To have your skin taken. To have your face removed. To watch someone else wear it."

The lights went out.

Complete darkness.

Total silence.

And then, from the darkness, a voice.

"Doctor Fang."

Close.

Too close.

"Let's trade."

Fang felt a hand touch his cheek.

Cold.

Dead.

The fingers traced the line of his jaw.

And then they began to pull.

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Skin in the Safe

The Taoist's beads stopped clicking.

For a long moment, no one moved. Mrs. Chen's face hung in that terrible half-smile, tears still wet on her cheeks. The operating room lights hummed. Fang's scalpel caught the glare.

Then the Taoist's phone rang.

It was an old sound. A Nokia ringtone from twenty years ago. The Taoist pulled the phone from his robe with the careful slowness of a man who knew exactly what he would find on the screen.

He looked at it.

His face went pale.

"They found the temple," he said.

Fang blinked. "What?"

"The temple. My temple. The one where I kept the unguided souls." The Taoist's voice was flat. Mechanical. Like he was reading a report. "Someone broke in. Took the remaining vessels. Smashed the containment circles."

"Who?"

The Taoist looked up. His eyes were old now. Not with age. With something that had been old for a very long time.

"Your patients, Doctor. All of them. Every woman you've treated in the last six months. They're gathered outside your clinic right now."

Fang stepped to the window. Pulled the blind.

The parking lot was full.

Not with cars. With women.

They stood in rows. Perfectly still. Perfectly silent. Their faces were smooth and young and beautiful. Their eyes were open but empty. They stared at the clinic like they were waiting for something.

"Thirty-seven of them," the Taoist said. "The same number of containers in your basement."

"How do you know about my basement?"

"Because I've been watching you for three months. You're not as careful as you think." The Taoist pocketed his phone. "Those women out there? They're not your patients anymore. They're vessels. Containers. The spirits you skinned have been growing inside them. Feeding on their life force. And now they're ready to hatch."

Mrs. Chen's face laughed again. The sound came from somewhere deep in her throat, vibrating through the skin that wasn't hers.

"Hatch is a good word," she said. "I like that."

Fang's scalpel hand trembled. "What happens when they hatch?"

The Taoist didn't answer.

Instead, he walked to Mrs. Chen's bedside. He looked down at her. Then he did something Fang didn't expect.

He sat down.

"I met a woman once," the Taoist said. "A long time ago. She came to me with a problem. She said her face didn't feel like her own. Said she looked in the mirror and saw a stranger." He paused. "I told her she was lucky. Most people spend their whole lives trying to become someone else. She'd already done it."

Mrs. Chen's face stopped smiling.

"What are you doing?" she whispered.

"Telling a story." The Taoist's voice was soft. Almost kind. "The woman asked me to help her find her original face. The one she'd lost. I told her I couldn't. Because she hadn't lost it. She'd sold it. Traded it for a newer model. A younger one. A prettier one."

He looked at Mrs. Chen's eyes. "Sound familiar?"

"I didn't—"

"You did. You went to a back-alley cosmetic surgeon. Paid him to give you a face that wasn't yours. And now that face wants to go home." The Taoist reached out. His fingers touched Mrs. Chen's cheek. "But here's the thing about faces. They remember. They remember the person they belonged to. They remember the life they lived. The love they felt. The pain they endured."

Mrs. Chen's body started to shake.

"She was a singer," the Taoist continued. "The girl whose skin you're wearing. She sang in karaoke bars. She was saving money to go to university. She had a boyfriend who bought her flowers every Friday. She was going to be someone."

"Stop," Mrs. Chen whispered.

"She was murdered. In a bathroom. By a man who wanted her face." The Taoist's voice hardened. "He cut it off while she was still alive. Took it to a doctor who traded in human skin. And that doctor glued it onto you."

Mrs. Chen's mouth opened. A sound came out. Not a scream. Not a sob. Something in between. Something that had been trapped for months.

"She's inside me," Mrs. Chen said. Her voice was breaking. "I can feel her. She's been there since the surgery. Watching. Waiting. She knows everything about me. She knows my daughter's name. She knows my husband's favorite song. She knows where I keep the spare key."

"She's not just inside you," the Taoist said. "She's becoming you. Every day, she takes a little more. And soon—"

"Soon there won't be enough of me left to fight."

The Taoist nodded.

Mrs. Chen's face started to cry. Real tears. Her own tears. "I didn't know. When I went to Doctor Fang, I didn't know where the skin came from. I thought it was synthetic. I thought—"

"You thought you could cheat time." The Taoist stood up. "But time doesn't cheat. It collects. And now it's come to collect from you."

He turned to Fang. "You have thirty minutes. Maybe less. Those women outside are going to wake up. And when they do, they're going to remember everything. They're going to remember the faces they lost. The lives they lived. The moment they were cut open and hollowed out."

"And then?"

"And then they're going to come inside. They're going to find your basement. They're going to open every container. And they're going to take back what belongs to them."

Fang's phone buzzed.

He looked at it.

*Doctor Fang, my skin is moving. It's trying to get off.*

Another message:

*Doctor Fang, I can hear her crying. She's inside my head. She won't stop.*

Another:

*Doctor Fang, she's showing me memories that aren't mine. I saw a man with a knife. I saw my own face on a table. I saw you holding it.*

Fang dropped the phone.

"How did they get my number?"

"Because they're part of you now," the Taoist said. "Every patient you've treated. Every face you've stolen. They're all connected. Through the skin. Through the blood. Through the screams you tried to forget."

Mrs. Chen sat up.

Her body moved wrong. The joints didn't bend the way they should. The muscles didn't pull the right way. It was like watching a puppet whose strings were being pulled by someone who had never seen a human before.

"I remember," she said. Her voice was layered now. Two voices. Hers and the singer's. "I remember everything. The bathroom. The knife. The way he talked to me while he cut."

She looked at Fang.

"His name was Wang. He wore a blue jacket. He smelled like cigarettes. He told me I had beautiful skin and he was sorry but he needed it more than I did."

Fang's stomach turned.

"You knew him," Mrs. Chen said. "He brought you my face in a Ziploc bag. You paid him five thousand yuan. You didn't ask questions."

The Taoist stepped between them. "That's enough."

"No," Mrs. Chen said. "It's not enough. It will never be enough."

She swung her legs off the bed. Stood up. Her body swayed. Her new face was calm now. Serene. Like a lake after a storm.

"I'm going to go outside," she said. "I'm going to join the others. And we're going to wait."

"For what?"

"For the doors to open. For the barriers to fall. For the moment when the skin remembers what it used to be."

She walked to the door. Her steps were steady now. Growing more human with each stride.

"Doctor Fang," she said. "I want you to watch. I want you to see what happens when thirty-seven women take back their faces all at once."

She left.

The door swung shut.

The Taoist turned to Fang. "The collection. Where is it?"

"Basement. Behind the refrigerator. There's a false wall."

"Show me."

Fang led him through the clinic. The hallways were dark. The emergency lights had kicked in. Red glow. Long shadows.

They passed the recovery rooms. Empty beds. Empty chairs. The patients had all gone outside.

They passed the consultation office. Fang's desk. His certificates. His photos with satisfied customers.

The Taoist stopped.

"This woman," he said, pointing to a photo. "When did you treat her?"

"Six months ago."

"She's dead now. The spirit took her completely three days ago. She walked into traffic while her husband watched."

Fang looked at the photo. The woman was smiling. Young. Beautiful. Happy.

"My wife," Fang said. "I didn't know."

"Of course you didn't know. You never asked. You never wanted to know what happened after they left your clinic." The Taoist grabbed Fang's arm. "Basement. Now."

The basement stairs were narrow. Concrete. The light was flickering.

At the bottom, the refrigerator hummed. Titanium racks. Glass containers.

The Taoist walked past it. Found the false wall. Pulled it open.

Behind it was a room.

Jars. Hundreds of them. Floating in formaldehyde. Pieces of skin. Whole faces. Fingers. Lips. Eyelids. All preserved. All waiting.

"My God," the Taoist whispered. "You've been doing this for years."

"People want to be beautiful," Fang said. "I gave them what they wanted."

"You gave them death."

The Taoist walked through the room. His beads clicked. His eyes moved from jar to jar.

"These spirits. They're all here. All trapped. All waiting."

"Waiting for what?"

The Taoist stopped at a jar. It was different from the others. Larger. Darker.

"You killed her in the karaoke bar," he said. "But she wasn't your first. She wasn't even your tenth."

He turned the jar.

Inside, a face floated. Eyes open. Mouth open. Frozen in a scream.

"This was your first," the Taoist said. "Twenty years ago. A college student. She came to you for a mole removal. You killed her in the chair. Took her whole face. Framed it."

Fang's hands were shaking.

"How do you know that?"

"Because she's been screaming in my dreams for nineteen years." The Taoist set the jar down. "I've been hunting you. Hunting this collection. Hunting the truth."

He looked at Fang.

"And now I've found it."

The lights flickered. The jars vibrated. The room grew cold.

From upstairs, a sound.

Footsteps. Many of them. Coming down the stairs.

The Taoist didn't move.

"They're here," he said. "And they're not going to leave anything behind."
